title: Mean, median and mode
intro: Imagine you run a pizzeria, "Mama John's", with seven employees — cooks, a cashier, couriers. Time to post a job opening, and you want to state the "typical salary". Only "typical" can mean different things, and the measure you pick changes both the wording of the posting and the candidate's expectations. Let's work through a live example: drag the points right on the chart and try the buttons beneath it.

beats.text: On the line: the salaries of seven employees, in thousands of dollars. They sit close together. Above them, three markers: the mean, the median and the mode. Drag any point (with a finger or a mouse) — and notice that all three markers move together with the data.
While the salaries cluster together, all three measures nearly coincide: the "typical" salary reads unambiguously — around 35k. No catch so far.
beats.predict: Now a director joins the pizzeria with a salary of 95k. Drag the rightmost point to the edge of the line. Which marker do you think will jump more: the mean or the median?
beats.reveal: The mean. It adds up all salaries and divides by the headcount, so one big paycheck pulls it upward. Drag the point to the right and compare the numbers under the line.

beats.text: The mean jumped, the median barely moved. That is the whole point: the mean cares about the size of every number, the median only about the order. It simply stands in the middle of the sorted row, and one rich person at the edge cannot move it.

Play with it yourself: hire new people with "add a point" — they come in on high salaries and pull the mean up every time, while the median barely flinches. "Remove a point" drops the last one. You can see the pattern: the mean chases the extreme values, while the median sticks with the "middle of the team".

The third measure, the mode, is the value that occurs most often (two points in the same spot). For salaries it is usually uninformative, but it is indispensable for categories: "the best-selling shoe size" or "the most common payment method".

Let's pull it together: the mean chases the extreme values, the median holds the middle of the row, and the mode shows the most frequent value. Which measure to take depends on the data: symmetric without outliers → the mean works, it uses all the information; a long tail or outliers (incomes, prices, time) → the median is more honest; categories ("which option is more popular") → only the mode. Below we'll unpack what this means in practice.

definitions.text: The sum of all values divided by their count. It uses the size of every number, so one large value noticeably pulls the mean toward itself.
definitions.simple: split the total evenly among everyone — every value contributes.

definitions.text: The value exactly in the middle of the row sorted in ascending order (with an even count, the average of the two central ones). It relies only on the order of values, not their size, so it is robust to outliers.
definitions.simple: the value from the middle of the row — rare extreme points don't move it.

definitions.text: The value that occurs more often than the rest. There can be one mode, several, or none — if all values are distinct.
definitions.simple: the most frequent value in the set.

practice: The average company salary is almost always higher than what most people earn: the top inflates it. One director's paycheck is enough for the mean to drift up, even though nothing changed for the rest of the staff.

That is why the "typical" income is more honestly shown by the median: it stands in the middle of the row, and lone rich outliers cannot move it. And the gap between the mean and the median is itself a handy signal: the larger it is, the more skewed the data.
realLife: At the scale of a whole country this is critical: when household incomes or housing prices are discussed, statistical agencies publish the median next to the mean — otherwise a few billionaires or a couple of luxury mansions would drag the mean so far up it would stop describing an ordinary family's life. So in salary news look for the word "median".

And the rule is wider than salaries: service response times, call durations, order sizes — wherever rare large values drag the mean along, the median is more honest. We'll meet this skew again when we get to distribution shapes.
nextLabel: We found the center — but how spread out are the salaries around it? Two teams with the same mean can live completely differently → spread and standard deviation.

related.label: Skewed data: when the mean lies
